questions.q: What does the Sanskrit word 'Ayurveda' broadly mean?
questions.explanation: Ayurveda combines 'ayur' (life) and 'veda' (knowledge or science), meaning the science or knowledge of life.

questions.q: Ayurveda originated in which country?
questions.explanation: Ayurveda is a traditional system of medicine that originated in the Indian subcontinent thousands of years ago.

questions.q: In Ayurveda, the three fundamental bodily energies are collectively called what?
questions.explanation: The three doshas (Vata, Pitta and Kapha) are the central concept of balance in Ayurvedic physiology.

questions.q: Which of the following is one of the three doshas?
questions.explanation: Vata, along with Pitta and Kapha, is one of the three doshas in Ayurveda.

questions.q: Ayurveda is traditionally regarded as connected to which group of ancient Indian scriptures?
questions.explanation: Ayurveda is traditionally linked to the Vedas, and is often associated as an upaveda (supplementary knowledge) of the Atharvaveda.

questions.q: Which classical Ayurvedic text is primarily associated with internal medicine and is attributed to Charaka?
questions.explanation: The Charaka Samhita is the foundational Ayurvedic text on internal medicine, named after the physician Charaka.

questions.q: Which classical text is especially renowned for its detailed descriptions of surgery in Ayurveda?
questions.explanation: The Sushruta Samhita, attributed to Sushruta, is famous for its detailed treatment of surgical techniques and instruments.

questions.q: Besides herbs and treatments, Ayurveda places major emphasis on which everyday factors for health?
questions.explanation: Ayurveda strongly emphasizes diet, daily routine and lifestyle as central to maintaining health and balance among the doshas.

questions.q: Ayurveda is best described as which of the following?
options: One of the world's oldest systems of medicine
questions.explanation: Ayurveda is one of the world's oldest continuously practised systems of medicine.

questions.q: In modern India, which government ministry oversees Ayurveda and other traditional systems of medicine?
questions.explanation: The Ministry of AYUSH oversees Ayurveda, Yoga, Unani, Siddha and Homoeopathy in India.

questions.q: Which dosha is primarily associated with the elements of air and space (ether) and governs movement?
questions.explanation: Vata is associated with air and ether (space) and is considered to govern movement and the nervous functions in Ayurveda.

questions.q: Which dosha is chiefly associated with fire and governs digestion and metabolism?
questions.explanation: Pitta is associated with fire (and water) and is considered to govern digestion, metabolism and transformation.

questions.q: Kapha dosha is primarily associated with which pair of elements?
questions.explanation: Kapha is associated with the earth and water elements and is linked to structure, stability and lubrication in the body.

questions.q: The classical Ayurvedic detoxification and purification therapy is known by what name?
questions.explanation: Panchakarma ('five actions') is the classical Ayurvedic set of purification and detoxification procedures.

questions.q: The 'tridosha' theory of Ayurveda refers to the balance among which three?
questions.explanation: The tridosha theory describes health as the proper balance among the three doshas: Vata, Pitta and Kapha.

questions.q: Sushruta, an ancient figure central to Ayurvedic surgery, is often honoured with which title?
questions.explanation: Sushruta is widely honoured as the Father of Surgery for the surgical knowledge recorded in the Sushruta Samhita.

questions.q: The Sushruta Samhita is especially noted for early descriptions of which reconstructive procedure?
questions.explanation: The Sushruta Samhita describes early techniques of rhinoplasty, reconstructing the nose using a flap of skin.

questions.q: In Ayurveda, an individual's unique inherent constitution or mind-body type is referred to as their what?
questions.explanation: Prakriti refers to a person's natural constitution, determined by the proportion of the doshas at birth.

questions.q: Which trio forms the classical 'Brihat Trayi' (Great Three) texts of Ayurveda?
options: Charaka Samhita, Sushruta Samhita, Ashtanga Hridaya
questions.explanation: The Brihat Trayi consists of the Charaka Samhita, the Sushruta Samhita and the Ashtanga Hridaya (of Vagbhata).

questions.q: The Ashtanga Hridaya, one of Ayurveda's classical texts, is traditionally attributed to which author?
questions.explanation: The Ashtanga Hridaya is attributed to Vagbhata and is one of the three great classical texts of Ayurveda.

questions.q: In Ayurvedic theory, the universe and body are made of five basic elements known as the Pancha Mahabhuta. Which is NOT one of them?
questions.explanation: The Pancha Mahabhuta are earth, water, fire, air and ether (space); metal is not among them.

questions.q: In Ayurveda, 'Agni' is a key concept referring to which bodily function?
questions.explanation: Agni in Ayurveda refers to the digestive fire responsible for digestion, absorption and metabolism.

questions.q: In Ayurveda, the metabolic and toxic waste residue believed to accumulate from poor digestion is called what?
questions.explanation: Ama refers to undigested or toxic residue thought to accumulate when digestion (agni) is impaired, contributing to disease.

questions.q: Ayurveda is classically organized into how many major branches or specialties?
questions.explanation: Classical Ayurveda is organized into eight branches, collectively known as Ashtanga Ayurveda (the eight limbs).

questions.q: In Ayurveda, the branch dealing with toxicology, poisons and their antidotes is known as what?
questions.explanation: Agada Tantra is the Ayurvedic branch concerned with toxicology, the treatment of poisons and bites.

questions.q: Within Ayurveda, the specialty of 'Rasayana' is concerned primarily with what?
questions.explanation: Rasayana is the Ayurvedic branch dealing with rejuvenation therapies aimed at promoting vitality, immunity and longevity.

questions.q: In Ayurveda, 'Dhatu' refers to which of the following?
questions.explanation: The seven Dhatus are the fundamental bodily tissues (such as rasa, rakta, mamsa and others) that sustain the body.

questions.q: Sushruta's surgical text classically describes roughly how many types of surgical instruments (yantra and shastra)?
questions.explanation: The Sushruta Samhita describes over a hundred surgical instruments, including blunt instruments (yantra) and sharp ones (shastra).

questions.q: The branch of Ayurveda known as 'Kaumarabhritya' deals with which area of medicine?
questions.explanation: Kaumarabhritya is the Ayurvedic branch dealing with pediatrics, child care and related obstetric matters.

questions.q: In Ayurveda, the subtle essence considered the basis of immunity, strength and vitality is called what?
questions.explanation: Ojas is regarded in Ayurveda as the subtle vital essence that underlies immunity, strength and overall vitality.
